headline: STOCKS SURGE AS GRAINS INCH HIGHER
subheadline: Broad-based gains suggest improving market confidence heading into peak planting season.
lead: Corn futures gained 1.25¢ to $4.50/bu while soybeans added 4.25¢ to $11.66/bu in quiet trade, as attention shifted to the S&P 500's 2.5% rally. The modest grain gains reflect steady underlying demand as planting season approaches, with weather remaining the key wild card.
teaser: Modest grain gains overshadowed by surging stocks as planting season looms.

one_number.context: Oil's push back toward $100 signals firming energy costs just as spring fieldwork ramps up. While still manageable, this trend threatens to squeeze margins if diesel demand spikes during peak planting operations across the Corn Belt.

sections.title: Grains & Oilseeds
sections.body: Corn futures gained 1.25¢ to $4.50/bu while soybeans added 4.25¢ to $11.66/bu in what traders called 'boring but bullish' action. December corn at $4.78/bu reflects modest carry that suggests adequate but not burdensome supplies. With optimal planting windows opening in 6 days, weather will soon dominate price discovery. Current levels represent solid seasonal support heading into the critical planting period.
sections.bottom_line: Steady gains reflect solid underlying demand as planting season approaches.
sections.farmer_action: Hold current positions and monitor 7-day weather forecasts — any planting delays could quickly shift price dynamics.

sections.title: Livestock & Dairy
sections.body: Live cattle gained 0.1% to $245.75 while feeders added 0.4% to $367.88, maintaining strong positioning near yearly highs. Lean hogs broke the trend, falling 2.3% to $104.65 in what appeared to be profit-taking after recent volatility. Cattle margins remain exceptionally strong with both cash and futures showing resilience. Class III milk slipped 0.2% to $17.06, reflecting normal seasonal pressure as spring flush approaches.
sections.bottom_line: Strong cattle margins persist while hog volatility continues.
sections.farmer_action: Cattle producers should consider locking some protection on these historically strong margins.

sections.title: Energy & Inputs
sections.body: WTI crude jumped 2.0% to $99.14, approaching the psychologically important $100 level as spring demand builds. Soybean meal gained 0.6% to $315.50 while natural gas held steady at $2.72. The energy sector's strength signals potential pressure on input costs just as fieldwork intensifies. Gold's 0.9% gain to $4,775 suggests broader inflationary concerns are returning to the fore.
sections.bottom_line: Rising energy costs threaten to squeeze margins during peak planting operations.
sections.farmer_action: Lock remaining diesel needs now — current levels are workable but vulnerable to spring demand spikes.

sections.title: Macro & Trade
sections.body: The S&P 500's 2.5% surge to 6,782.81 dominated market attention, suggesting improving risk appetite across asset classes. The dollar index held steady at exactly $100.00, maintaining its recent range. Broad market strength often translates to improved commodity demand as economic confidence builds. Bitcoin's modest 0.1% gain to $71,188 shows crypto taking a backseat to traditional assets in this rally.
sections.bottom_line: Surging stocks signal improved risk appetite that could benefit commodities.
sections.farmer_action: Monitor broader market momentum — sustained equity strength often supports commodity demand.

the_more_you_know.title: Why April Weather Windows Matter Most
the_more_you_know.body: Every day of corn planting delay after April 15 costs roughly 1 bushel per acre in yield potential. This isn't farmer superstition — it's backed by decades of university research. The biological reason: corn needs specific heat units to reach maturity before fall frost. Late planting compresses that timeline and forces the plant to endure summer heat stress during critical pollination. This is why weather forecasts become market-moving events starting mid-April.

watch_list.desc: Any rain systems threatening April 15-20 planting window

daily_quote.text: There are no shortcuts in farming. Just long days and early mornings.

source_summary: CBOT futures, CME livestock, NYMEX energy, major equity indices
